---
title: "Principled Default Priors for Bayesian Hypothesis Testing in Binomial GLMs: A Matched-Prior Framework with Empirical Evaluation"
shorttitle: "Matched Priors for BF Testing in Binomial GLMs"
author:
  - name: Gidon T. Frischkorn
    orcid: 0000-0002-5055-9764
    email: gidon.frischkorn@psychologie.uzh.ch
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Zurich
        department: Department of Psychology
  - name: Joscha Dutli
    orcid: 0009-0009-5007-1281
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Zurich
        department: Department of Psychology
  - name: Philipp Musfeld
    orcid: 0000-0002-6539-0105
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Amsterdam
        department: Department of Psychology
      - name: University of Zurich
        department: Department of Psychology
  - name: Klaus Oberauer
    orcid: 0000-0003-3902-7318
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Zurich
        department: Department of Psychology
author-note:
  disclosures:
    study-registration: "This study was not preregistered."
    data-sharing: "Simulation code and analysis scripts are available at https://github.com/GidonFrischkorn/PriorsBinomial; pre-computed simulation outputs are archived on the Open Science Framework (https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/YJGKT)."
    conflict-of-interest: "The author(s) report having no conflicts of interest with respect to contents, authorship, or publication of this article."
    financial-support: "This research was supported by a grant from the Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF) awarded to Gidon T. Frischkorn (Grant No. 208828)."
    authorship-agreements: |
      Gidon T. Frischkorn: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal Analysis, Visualization, Writing -- Original Draft, Writing -- Review & Editing.
      Joscha Dutli: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing -- Review & Editing.
      Philipp Musfeld: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing -- Review & Editing.
      Klaus Oberauer: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology, Writing -- Original Draft, Writing -- Review & Editing.
floatsintext: true
output-dir: "."
mask: false
masked-citations:
  - oberauer2019
  - oberauer2022
  - oberauer2026bf
  - frischkorn2026preprint
format:
  apaquarto-docx: default
  apaquarto-html: default
  apaquarto-pdf:
    documentmode: man
bibliography:
  - references.bib
keywords:
  - generalized linear models
  - prior specification
  - binomial models
  - Bayes factor
  - Bayesian statistics
abstract: "Researchers using Bayesian hypothesis testing in binomial generalized linear models (GLMs; for instance, logistic regression for accuracy data) need priors on regression coefficients, but no widely adopted default framework exists. The nonlinear link function creates a prior-link interaction: priors that appear reasonable on the coefficient scale can imply implausible distributions on the probability scale. We show that this problem has an exact solution for CDF-based link functions: *matched priors* -- prior families whose CDF mirrors the link function -- produce uniform distributions on the probability scale for the intercept, a maximally uninformative anchor. We extend this family choice to effect priors for parsimony, with prior scale determined via prior predictive simulation. We embed this matched-prior principle in a three-step workflow and evaluate it via simulation: (1) identify matched priors, (2) evaluate prior predictive distributions, (3) calibrate Bayes factors and compare to the JZS/Cauchy default. For logistic regression, the matched Logistic(0, 0.75) intercept prior and matched Logistic effect priors with scale 0.25 to 0.30 provide well-calibrated Bayes factors. A signal detection analysis shows that prior scale is a criterion parameter: it shifts the operating point on the ROC curve but does not change discriminability. The advantage of the matched-prior workflow is principled scale selection on the probability scale, where effect sizes are directly interpretable. The workflow extends naturally to hierarchical models, where the prior on the random-effects variance has negligible influence on Bayes factors for fixed effects. Ready-to-use `brms` code and scaling formulas for any contrast coding are provided."
abstract-public: "When psychologists use Bayesian analysis to test whether an experimental result is real -- for example, whether a memory training improved accuracy -- they must supply the software with a prior distribution that describes what effect sizes they consider plausible before seeing the data. For the most common type of outcome in laboratory experiments (how often participants respond correctly), no standard guidance has existed for choosing these distributions when using logistic regression. This matters because a poorly chosen prior can cause the software to give unreliable evidence, particularly for small effects. This paper provides clear default recommendations. The key idea is simple: because logistic regression uses a specific mathematical function to convert model parameters to probabilities, there is a natural family of distributions -- the logistic family -- that produces a completely neutral (uniform) starting assumption on the probability scale. We call these 'matched priors.' Starting from this neutral anchor, we show through extensive simulations which specific parameter values yield well-behaved Bayes factors across the range of sample sizes and effect sizes typical in psychological research. Critically, prior scale functions as a decision threshold: it shifts where the Bayes factor falls relative to any fixed criterion but does not change how well it discriminates true effects from null. The advantage of the matched-prior approach is principled scale selection: choosing the prior on the probability scale, where effect sizes are directly interpretable. Ready-to-use code for the brms R package is provided so that researchers can apply the recommendations without technical expertise in Bayesian computation."
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggridges)
library(viridis)
library(scales)
library(here)
library(knitr)

source(here("R", "link_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "prior_sampling.R"))
source(here("R", "plotting.R"))

# Goal 1: Prior predictive results
summaries_pp  <- readRDS(here("output", "prior_predictive_summaries.rds"))
draws_long    <- readRDS(here("output", "prior_predictive_draws.rds"))

# Goal 2: BF calibration results
summaries_bf  <- readRDS(here("output", "bf_calibration_summaries.rds"))

re_summaries <- tryCatch(
  readRDS(here("output", "bf_calibration_re_summaries.rds")),
  error = function(e) NULL
)
re_results_available <- !is.null(re_summaries)

# Goal 2 extended: JZS comparison results
jzs_summaries <- tryCatch(
  readRDS(here("output", "bf_calibration_jzs_summaries.rds")),
  error = function(e) NULL
)
jzs_available <- !is.null(jzs_summaries)

# Pre-computed figures from local/ scripts
roc_dual_fig_available <- file.exists(here("figures", "roc_dual_comparison.png"))
auc_fig_available      <- file.exists(here("figures", "auc_jzs_comparison.png"))

# Factor labels
dist_labels <- c(cauchy = "Cauchy", normal = "Normal", logistic = "Logistic")

# Use prior_theme() from R/plotting.R for all figures
```

## Introduction

In psychology and related fields, we often have data that reflect counts of successes out of trials -- for instance, accuracy in a cognitive task or endorsement rates in a survey. These data follow a binomial distribution and are best modeled with a binomial generalized linear model (GLM):

$$k_i \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n_i, p_i)$$

Here, the number of successes $k_i$ is the result of $n_i$ trials given the success probability $p_i$. To assess any effects on the number of successes, the success probability $p_i$ is related to a linear prediction term via a link function $g$ to accommodate the bounded nature of probabilities:

$$g(p_i) = b_0 + b_1 x_i$$

To translate the result of the linear prediction term back to the probability scale, the inverse of the link-function must be applied:

$$p_i = g^{-1}(b_0 + b_1 x_i)$$

For binomial models, the inverse of the link function $g^{-1}$ is either the logistic function (logistic regression), or the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the standard normal distribution (probit regression).

When testing the hypothesis that an effect of interest exists, Bayesian hypothesis testing via Bayes factors [@wagenmakers2018a; @schonbrodt2018; @heck2022] compares a model including the effect ($H_1$) against one constraining it to zero ($H_0$); the Bayes factor (BF) $BF_{10}$ quantifies how much more likely the observed data are under $H_1$ than under $H_0$. Because $H_1$ is characterized by the prior on the effect parameter, the BF depends directly on what effects the prior treats as plausible. For models assuming normally distributed outcomes, @rouder2012 proposed principled default priors based on the JZS (Jeffreys--Zellner--Siow) framework, encoding plausible standardized effect sizes in a scale-invariant manner.

Priors in Bayesian statistics can serve multiple purposes: encoding knowledge about plausible effect sizes, regularizing estimates to prevent overfitting, or stabilizing inference when data are sparse. For posterior estimation, weakly informative priors such as the Cauchy(0, 2.5) recommended by @gelman2008 -- a scale that assumes continuous predictors centered and scaled to a standard deviation of 0.5, and binary predictors coded to $\pm 0.5$ -- serve the regularization goal well: they prevent extreme estimates while allowing the data to dominate at reasonable sample sizes. For BF-based hypothesis testing, however, the prior does more than regularize -- it defines the alternative hypothesis $H_1$. Accordingly, the prior should encode what effects are plausible in the research domain; a prior that assigns mass to implausible effects biases the BF toward $H_0$ even when a genuine small effect is present [@hoijtink2022; @schonbrodt2018].

The difficulty for generalized linear models arises because the parameters of interest are estimated on the transformed, unbounded scale on which the regression coefficients are defined. Accordingly, priors must be expressed on the coefficient scale for model estimation, whereas our considerations regarding plausible effects -- and the constraints arising from the bounded nature of probability -- refer to the probability scale (or native outcome scale for other types of GLMs). This creates a prior-link interaction: a prior that appears reasonable on the regression-coefficient scale can produce implausible distributions on the probability scale [@gelman2008; @ghosh2018]. In addition, priors recommended for posterior estimation [e.g., the weakly informative Cauchy(0, 2.5) of @gelman2008] are typically too broad for hypothesis testing, biasing Bayes factors toward the null. For GLMs with nonlinear link functions, no principled defaults have been widely adopted in psychology for Bayesian hypothesis testing.

We propose a three-step workflow for prior specification in GLMs and refer to it as the matched-prior workflow. Anchoring prior choice on the probability scale is itself not new: conditional mean priors [@bedrick1996] specify prior beliefs directly on response probabilities and translate them to the coefficient scale. What has been missing is a *default* that requires no elicitation -- a fixed, design-independent prior specification suitable for Bayes factor hypothesis testing -- and a workflow for selecting and checking its scale. While we demonstrate the workflow fully for binomial models, the same challenge arises whenever a link function mediates between the coefficient scale and the native outcome scale -- ordinal regression for Likert responses, lognormal regression for response times, Poisson models for count data -- and the three steps apply in each case. Here, we provide default priors and ready-to-use code for both the flat and the hierarchical random-intercept case.

## Related Work {#sec-related-work}

Before presenting the matched-prior workflow in detail, we situate it within the two bodies of existing work most directly relevant to it: the g-prior tradition underlying the JZS/Cauchy default that dominates Bayesian hypothesis testing in psychology, and conditional mean priors with structured elicitation. This contextualizes what the matched-prior framework contributes -- and where it differs from available alternatives.

### The G-Prior Tradition: From Linear Models to GLMs

@liang2008 established mixtures of g-priors as a principled, analytically tractable framework for default priors in linear regression. In this approach, priors for a model parameter $\beta$ are specified as $\beta \sim \mathrm{Normal}\!\left(0,\, g \cdot (X'X)^{-1}/\phi\right)$, so the prior covariance scales with the data covariance matrix through the hyperparameter $g$.[^gnotation] Concretely, $X'X$ is the cross-product matrix of the design matrix, whose diagonal entries are proportional to the variance of each predictor. Its inverse $(X'X)^{-1}$ is correspondingly smaller for high-variance predictors (yielding a tighter prior), so the g-prior adapts its width to the information content of the design. For a balanced two-group experiment with sum-to-zero $\pm 1$ coding, $X'X/n = I$ and the prior simplifies to $\beta \sim \mathrm{Normal}(0,\, g\sigma^2 I)$: every coefficient receives the same prior width, controlled only by $g$ and $\sigma^2$. Rather than fixing $g$ (a choice that remains largely arbitrary), the mixture approach treats $g$ as random and marginalizes over it. The key insight is that placing an Inverse-Gamma prior on $g$ (equivalently, a Gamma prior on the precision $1/g$) and integrating out $g$ yields Cauchy-distributed marginals for $\beta$: a Normal prior with Gamma-distributed variance is a scale mixture of normals that integrates to a Cauchy. The Zellner--Siow prior (originally Zellner & Siow, 1980, as represented by @liang2008) formalizes this by setting $g \sim \mathrm{Inverse\text{-}Gamma}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2},\, \tfrac{n}{2}\right)$, giving multivariate Cauchy marginals for $\beta$ centered at zero. This Normal-variance-mixture construction provides the formal justification for the Cauchy default priors widely adopted in Bayesian hypothesis testing [e.g., @rouder2009]. @rouder2012 showed that this framework achieves genuine design independence for linear analysis of variance: by placing priors on *standardized* effects $\delta = \beta / \sigma$ (that is, setting $\beta \mid g, \sigma \sim \mathrm{Normal}(0,\, g\sigma^2 I)$ combined with a Jeffreys prior $\pi(\mu, \sigma^2) \propto 1/\sigma^2$), the prior covariance reduces to $G = gI$, independent of the design matrix and any data properties. This holds because sum-to-zero and orthonormal contrast codings make $X'X$ proportional to the identity matrix; after $\sigma$-standardization, the design-specific scaling factors cancel and the prior width becomes uniform across all coefficients. Under sum-to-zero or orthonormal contrast coding, the resulting Bayes factor therefore depends only on the amount of variance explained by all predictors $R^2$ and is invariant to linear transformations of the outcome and to the specific design.

[^gnotation]: The symbol $g$ has two distinct meanings in this paper, each standard in its own literature: the link function $g(\cdot)$ of a GLM, and the scalar hyperparameter $g$ of a g-prior. The two are unrelated. We retain both conventions because each is the established notation in its field; the intended meaning is always clear from context, as the link function appears only as $g(\cdot)$ applied to a probability or linear predictor.

The design independence achieved by @rouder2012 rests on two structural features of Gaussian linear models that do not carry over to binomial GLMs. First, linear models have a free scale parameter $\sigma^2$: by expressing effects on the standardized scale $\delta = \beta / \sigma$, both the prior and the resulting Bayes factor become invariant to measurement units of the outcome. Binomial GLMs (and many other GLM types) have no separate variance parameter: the variance $p(1-p)$ is entirely determined by the mean, so there is no $\sigma$ to standardize against. Second, the identity link in linear models means a scale-invariant prior on $\beta$ translates directly into a scale-invariant distribution over outcome differences. With a nonlinear link function, the mapping from $\beta$ to the probability scale is distorted by the link's curvature in a way that depends on the baseline probability, and therefore on the design and data. 

To address this issue, @bove2011 and @liclyde2018 extended the g-prior framework to binomial GLMs by substituting a version of the Fisher information matrix for $(X'X)$, which yields approximate marginal likelihoods (via Laplace approximation) and thus enables principled Bayes factor estimation. This substitution, however, reintroduces design dependence: the GLM Fisher information is built from the binomial variance $p_i(1-p_i)$ evaluated at each predicted probability, so the prior's implied scale tracks those predicted probabilities and therefore shifts with the baseline probability, the design, and the data. In plain terms, the same nominal g-prior implies a different prior on the probability scale for every dataset, so no universal scale recommendation such as "$\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 0.75)$" is possible. We work through this weight-matrix algebra and its consequences in detail in the supplement (see Comparison to G-Priors in the Supplementary Materials). @sekulovski2026 provide a recent large-scale comparison of Bayesian variable selection methods for logistic regression, including g-prior-based approaches, and @porwalraftery2022 provide a broader comparison of Bayesian and frequentist model selection methods across a range of regression settings.

Yet even a hypothetical design-independent g-prior for GLMs would leave a second problem unresolved: it would not inform us what the prior implies on the probability scale. A prior that appears reasonable on the unbounded coefficient scale can assign substantial mass to probabilities near 0 or 1, implausible for most behavioral experiments [@gelman2008; @ghosh2018]. This *prior--link interaction* is precisely the problem the matched-prior framework aims to address: by anchoring prior choice on the probability scale via the probability integral transform, a single scale recommendation applies across all datasets with the same link function, and prior predictive simulation provides a directly interpretable diagnostic. For linear models, the dominant Cauchy default priors in Bayesian hypothesis testing [@rouder2009; @rouder2012] are precisely this operationalization: Cauchy-distributed marginals arise from the Normal-variance-mixture construction applied to standardized effects $\delta = \beta/\sigma$. This standardization requires a free variance parameter $\sigma$, which is unavailable in binomial GLMs, where the variance is fully determined by the mean. In practice, the Cauchy default is therefore applied as independent Cauchy priors placed directly on the regression coefficients, without standardization, as proposed by @oberauer2019 for logistic regression, motivated by the JZS t-test scale convention [@rouder2009].[^gprior]

[^gprior]: A mathematical comparison of the g-prior and matched-prior frameworks is provided in the supplementary materials. Note also that g-priors themselves are not readily available in `brms` [@burkner2017; @burkner2018] or Stan, limiting their practical uptake in psychological research.

### Conditional Mean Priors and Prior Elicitation

@bedrick1996 introduced conditional mean priors for GLMs, in which the researcher specifies prior distributions on $P(Y=1 \mid x)$ at selected design points (that is, at chosen values of the predictor $x$, such as the two condition means in a two-group experiment); the prior on $\beta$ is then implied by a change-of-variables argument -- specifically, by applying the inverse link function to map the specified probability-scale distribution onto the coefficient scale. This approach is principled, directly interpretable on the probability scale, and has been extended in @christensen2010 and more recently by @hosack2026 for a broad family of GLMs. Simulation-based approaches to prior elicitation [@bockting2024] and reviews of elicitation methodology and its practical challenges [@stefan2021; @mikkola2024] further enrich this tradition.

The matched-prior approach proposed here offers a design-independent alternative to conditional mean priors. Conditional mean priors require the researcher to specify design points and probability distributions over expected outcomes at those points. These priors are semi-informative and elicitation-based, suited to situations where genuine prior knowledge is available. The matched prior is a *default* requiring no elicitation, appropriate when such knowledge is unavailable. Conditional mean priors were also developed primarily for parameter estimation rather than for Bayes factor hypothesis testing, which imposes the further requirement of a proper prior on $\beta$ under $H_1$ with a point mass at zero under $H_0$. The matched-prior workflow is designed specifically for this hypothesis-testing context. The insight that the probability scale provides an interpretable basis for prior specification is shared by both approaches: whereas computational fitting requires priors to be expressed on the coefficient scale, the *motivation* for prior choice can -- and we argue *should* -- come from the probability scale. Following the taxonomy of @mikkola2024, both approaches operate in the observable space, using the link function to bridge between prior distributions on the coefficient scale and their implications on the probability scale.

## The Three-Step Workflow {#sec-workflow}

Prior specification for GLMs with nonlinear link functions requires bridging the gap between the coefficient scale (where priors are set) and the probability scale (where effects are interpretable). Our workflow instantiates the principled Bayesian workflow for cognitive science [@schad2021] in the specific context of prior selection for Bayes factor testing, and proceeds through three complementary steps:

**Step 1: Identify matched priors.** Using the probability integral transform, we identify prior families whose cumulative distribution function (CDF) matches the inverse link function. For the intercept, this matched prior produces exactly a uniform distribution on the probability scale -- a principled, maximally uninformative anchor. We apply the same distributional family to effect priors for parsimony; the appropriate scale for all priors is then determined in Steps 2 and 3.

**Step 2: Prior predictive simulation.** Building on the matched priors, we draw samples from the joint prior on all coefficients while varying the prior scale, transform through the inverse link, and examine the resulting distributions on the probability scale. This reveals what effect sizes and baseline rates the prior treats as plausible -- information that is not intuitively apparent from the prior on the coefficient scale alone [@gelman2020].

**Step 3: Bayes factor calibration.** Finally, we simulate data under known effects and evaluate how the strength of evidence varies across prior specifications. This verifies that the priors recommended based on the prior predictive simulations yield well-calibrated Bayes factors -- neither systematically favoring $H_0$ nor $H_1$ -- and that they satisfy formal properties of principled Bayesian model comparison.

The three steps differ in how often a researcher performs them. Steps 1 and 2 are carried out in any given application: Step 1 selects the prior *family* from the mathematical structure of the link function, and Step 2 calibrates its *scale* on the probability scale. Step 3 is a one-time validation -- establishing that the resulting priors behave consistently with validated g-priors and standard criteria for Bayesian model comparison -- which subsequent studies can cite to justify their priors rather than repeat for every analysis. Taken together, the steps offer a principled alternative to the default g-prior route that currently dominates prior specification for GLMs.

Each step can be applied to any GLM family. Here, we demonstrate the full workflow for binomial models.

## Model Specification {#sec-model}

Before presenting each step in detail, we specify the model used throughout the paper: a hierarchical random-intercept binomial GLM. This introduces the parameters whose priors the three workflow steps will determine, and one additional prior component absent from a flat model: the hyperprior on the random-effects standard deviation $\sigma_u$.

Psychological experiments typically yield nested data: each participant contributes multiple trials, so observations are not independent across participants. We therefore extend the binomial GLM introduced earlier to a random-intercept model. Using the general link function $g$ introduced above, the complete hierarchical model is:

$$y_{ij} \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n_{ij},\, p_{ij})$$

$$g(p_{ij}) = b_0 + u_j + b_1 x_{ij}$$

where $j = 1, \ldots, J$ indexes participants and $i = 1, \ldots, n_j$ indexes trials within participant $j$. The participant-level random intercept $u_j \sim \mathrm{Normal}(0, \sigma_u^2)$ captures between-participant variability in baseline success probability, and $\sigma_u$ is the random-effects standard deviation. The link functions are the same as in the model introduced at the beginning: $g(p) = \log(p/(1-p))$ for logistic regression and $g(p) = \Phi^{-1}(p)$ for probit regression, where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the quantile function of the standard normal distribution. Participants may further differ in the *magnitude* of the experimental effect, motivating an additional random slope on $x_{ij}$; omitting such slopes when present in the data can substantially inflate false-positive rates in Bayesian hypothesis testing [@oberauer2022]. The present workflow focuses on the random-intercept model as the simplest hierarchical extension that introduces the key additional prior choice -- the hyperprior on $\sigma_u$.

Throughout the paper, we illustrate the workflow with a running example: accuracy data from a hypothetical experiment with two conditions. The predictor $x_i$ is coded with sum-to-zero contrasts $x_i \in \{+1, -1\}$, so that the experimental effect on the probability scale is $\delta p = g^{-1}(b_0 + b_1) - g^{-1}(b_0 - b_1)$, where $g^{-1}(\cdot)$ is the inverse link function.

The complete prior specification is:

- **Fixed effects:** $b_0 \sim \mathrm{Logistic}(0, \mathrm{sd}_{b_0})$ (logit) or $\mathrm{Normal}(0, \mathrm{sd}_{b_0})$ (probit), matched to the link function; $b_1 \sim \mathrm{Logistic}(0, \mathrm{sd}_{b_1})$ or $\mathrm{Normal}(0, \mathrm{sd}_{b_1})$
- **Random effects:** $u_j \sim \mathrm{Normal}(0, \sigma_u^2)$ with hyperprior $\sigma_u \sim \mathrm{Exponential}(\lambda)$

The scale of each prior ($\mathrm{sd}_{b_0}$, $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1}$, and the hyperprior rate $\lambda$) is left open here and examined systematically in Steps 2 and 3: we vary each scale across a wide range and let the prior predictive checks and Bayes factor calibration identify well-behaved values. The resulting recommended defaults are summarized later (see [Recommendations](#sec-recommendations)).

The Bayes factor for the fixed effect $b_1$ compares:

$$H_1:\; b_1 \neq 0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_0:\; b_1 = 0$$

Under both models, the random intercepts $u_j$ are present and integrated out in the marginal likelihoods used to compute the BF. Because $\sigma_u$ appears symmetrically in both marginal likelihoods, its hyperprior largely cancels from the ratio, leaving the BF dominated by the fixed-effect signal-to-noise ratio. This *nuisance-parameter robustness* means that the choice of hyperprior on $\sigma_u$ has little influence on BFs for the fixed effect $b_1$, provided a reasonable weakly informative prior is used.

## Step 1: The Matched Prior Principle {#sec-matched-prior}

The key mathematical principle underlying this approach is the following: for any continuous cumulative distribution function (CDF) $F$ with location 0 and scale 1, if $B_0 \sim F(\cdot;\, 0, 1)$, then $p = F(B_0) \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$ [@casella2002]. This follows from the probability integral transform: applying a distribution's own CDF to a draw from that distribution maps the draw to its percentile rank within the distribution, and percentile ranks are, by definition, uniformly distributed. Consequently, a $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 1)$ prior on $b_0$ combined with the logistic inverse link $g^{-1} = F_\mathrm{logistic}$ yields $p = F_\mathrm{logistic}(b_0) \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$; the same logic gives $\mathrm{Normal}(0, 1) \rightarrow \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$ under the probit link.

Stated in practical terms: when the prior distribution on the coefficient scale is matched exactly to the link function -- same family and same parameters (location 0, scale 1) -- it produces a flat (uninformative) prior on the probability scale. For binomial GLMs that either use the logit or the probit link, the matched prior is the Logistic(0, 1) or Normal(0, 1), respectively:

| Link   | Inverse link $g^{-1}$ | Matched prior | Implied prior on $p$ |
|--------|-----------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| logit  | logistic CDF | $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 1)$ | $\mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$ |
| probit | normal CDF | $\mathrm{Normal}(0, 1)$ | $\mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$  |

: Matched priors for binomial GLMs. By the probability integral transform, matching the prior family to the link function produces a uniform prior on the probability scale. {#tbl-matched-priors}

In other words, when we transform a $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 1)$ prior on the coefficient scale back to the probability scale via the inverse logit function, the result is a uniform distribution on $(0, 1)$; the same holds for a $\mathrm{Normal}(0, 1)$ prior under the probit link. The matched prior at scale 1 is therefore maximally uninformative on the probability scale -- a principled anchor from which we can obtain desired priors through scaling. Scaling the matched prior with $\mathrm{sd} < 1$ concentrates mass away from 0 and 1, producing a regularized intercept prior (one shrunk toward the center of the probability scale) suited to experiments where floor and ceiling effects are unlikely. Conversely, scaling with $\mathrm{sd} > 1$ concentrates mass toward 0 and 1, reflecting the typically unreasonable assumption that extreme baseline probabilities are particularly likely. This principle generalizes to any model with a CDF-based link function (see [Applying the Workflow Beyond Binomial Models](#sec-extensions)).

The probability integral transform argument developed here applies directly to the intercept prior, because the implied probability $g^{-1}(b_0)$ depends on $b_0$ alone. For effect priors, the same distributional family is adopted for parsimony -- a single family across all coefficients -- but the probability integral transform does not extend in the same way. This is because the sum of two Logistic random variables is not Logistic: the Logistic distribution is not closed under addition. Consequently, $b_0 + b_1$ (which determines the linear predictor in each condition) does not follow a Logistic distribution even when both $b_0$ and $b_1$ do, and the probability integral transform does not apply to $\delta p$ directly. The Normal distribution *is* closed under addition, so for the probit link the extension is more natural: $b_0 + b_1$ remains Normal and the probability integral transform logic carries over in a modified sense. For the logit link, the family choice for $b_1$ is therefore motivated by consistency with the intercept prior and validated empirically in Steps 2 and 3.

## Step 2: Prior Predictive Simulation {#sec-prior-predictive}

We simulated prior predictive distributions for Binomial GLMs across a factorial design crossing two link functions (logit, probit), three prior families for both intercept and effect coefficients (Normal, Logistic, and Cauchy), and a range of prior scale values. The Normal and Logistic families were included because each matches one of the two link functions; the Cauchy was included because @rouder2012 proposed Cauchy-distributed default priors for linear models, and [one of us]{.content-visible unless-meta="mask"}[prior work]{.content-visible when-meta="mask"} [@oberauer2019] has proposed them also for logistic models. The intercept prior SD ($\mathrm{sd}_{b_0}$) ranged from 0.50 to 1.50; the effect prior SD ($\mathrm{sd}_{b_1}$) ranged from 0.10 to 2.00, yielding 720 factorial combinations in total (see Supplementary Materials for the complete design specification). For each condition, we drew 10,000 samples from the joint prior on $(b_0, b_1)$ and computed the implied distributions on the probability scale.[^software]

[^software]: All simulations were conducted in R 4.5.2 [@r2023] using the SimDesign package [version 2.25; @chalmers2020]. Bayesian models for Step 3 were fitted with brms [version 2.23.0; @burkner2017; @burkner2018] using CmdStan [version 2.38.0; @stan2023] via the cmdstanr interface [version 0.9.0; @gabry2024]. Marginal likelihoods for bridge sampling were estimated with the bridgesampling package [version 1.2.1; @gronau2017]. [All simulation code and analysis scripts are available at <https://github.com/GidonFrischkorn/PriorsBinomial>.]{.content-visible unless-meta="mask"}[All simulation code and analysis scripts are available in a public repository (link withheld for masked review).]{.content-visible when-meta="mask"}

### Intercept Prior {#sec-pp-intercept}

The intercept $b_0$ determines the baseline success probability before any experimental manipulation. A poorly chosen intercept prior can place substantial mass near $p = 0$ or $p = 1$, predicting that participants perform at floor or ceiling even before any experimental effect is applied.

```{r}
#| label: fig-1-p-intercept
#| fig-cap: "Prior predictive distribution of success probability
#|   p = $g^{-1}(b_0)$ for each intercept prior SD (rows) and link function
#|   (columns). At $\\mathrm{sd}_{b_0} = 1.0$, the matched prior (Logistic for logit,
#|   Normal for probit) produces a perfectly uniform distribution. Reducing
#|   $\\mathrm{sd}_{b_0}$ to 0.75 pulls mass away from floor and ceiling. Dashed
#|   curves show exact analytical densities of the matched distribution family."
#| fig-height: 10
#| fig-width: 10

fig1_draws <- draws_long |>
  filter(dist_b1 == "logistic", sd_b1 == 0.25)

plot_p_intercept_ridges(fig1_draws, sd_b1_focus = 0.25)
```

@fig-1-p-intercept confirms the analytical result from the previous step: at $\mathrm{sd}_{b_0} = 1.0$ with the matched prior, the distribution of $p$ is uniform. At $\mathrm{sd}_{b_0} = 0.75$, mass is pulled away from 0 and 1, appropriate for experiments avoiding floor and ceiling effects. The Cauchy, which does not match either link, produces heavier tails. We recommend $\mathrm{sd}_{b_0} = 0.75$ as the default, that is, $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 0.75)$ for the logit link and $\mathrm{Normal}(0, 0.75)$ for the probit link (see the Supplementary Figures in the Supplementary Materials for a quantification of how much probability mass each prior specification assigns to the floor/ceiling regions).

### Effect Prior {#sec-pp-effect}

The Logistic family is used for $b_1$ by parsimony -- the same family as the intercept -- rather than by a direct probability integral transform derivation; the appropriate scale is determined from the prior predictive distributions below.

The prior on effect parameter $b_1$ determines how much prior mass falls on small versus large effects. Due to the nonlinear link, we must compute the implied prior on $\delta p$ by drawing from the joint prior on $(b_0, b_1)$ and transforming through the inverse link. Concretely, the implied prior on $\delta p$ is computed as $\delta p = g^{-1}(b_0 + b_1) - g^{-1}(b_0 - b_1)$. @fig-3-delta-p shows how the choice of $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1}$ translates into implied experimental effects in $\delta p$, holding the intercept prior fixed at the matched recommendation ($\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 0.75)$ for logit; $\mathrm{Normal}(0, 0.75)$ for probit).

```{r}
#| label: fig-3-delta-p
#| fig-cap: "Prior predictive distribution of $\\delta p$ (signed effect size on
#|   the probability scale) for each effect prior SD (facets), color-coded by
#|   prior family (ridges). Shown for the logit link with matched Logistic(0, 0.75)
#|   intercept prior. At $\\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.25$, Normal and Logistic priors concentrate
#|   mass on effects up to about 30 percentage points. The Cauchy assigns
#|   substantial probability to effects exceeding 50 percentage points even at
#|   this moderate scale. Dotted lines mark +/-0.10, +/-0.20, +/-0.30."
#| fig-height: 12
#| fig-width: 14

fig2_draws <- draws_long |>
  filter(
    link == "logit",
    dist_b0 == "logistic",
    sd_b0 == 0.75
  )

plot_delta_p_ridges(fig2_draws, sd_b0_focus = 0.75)
```

The Cauchy prior stands apart at every scale: even at $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.25$, its heavy tails assign substantial probability to $|\delta p| > 0.50$. Note that effects of $|\delta p| \approx 1.0$ would require perfect accuracy in one condition and zero correct responses in the other -- a scenario that is virtually impossible in practice. As Step 3 will show, this matters less for the BF's ability to discriminate $H_1$ from $H_0$ -- which is governed by the prior's scale -- than for the interpretability of the prior itself: the heavy tails make the effective spread on the probability scale hard to anticipate from the nominal scale parameter.

```{r}
#| label: setup-pp-effect-stats
#| include: false

# Summary statistics for effect prior plausibility comparison
# JZS Cauchy(0, sqrt(2)/2) ~ 0.707; closest grid value is 0.75
pp_effect_stats <- draws_long |>
  filter(link == "logit", dist_b0 == "logistic", sd_b0 == 0.75,
         sd_b1 %in% c(0.25, 0.75)) |>
  group_by(dist_b1, sd_b1) |>
  summarise(
    p90       = quantile(abs_delta_p, 0.90),
    prop_gt30 = mean(abs_delta_p > 0.30) * 100,
    .groups   = "drop"
  )

p90_logistic_25  <- pp_effect_stats |>
  filter(dist_b1 == "logistic", sd_b1 == 0.25) |> pull(p90)
prop_logistic_25 <- pp_effect_stats |>
  filter(dist_b1 == "logistic", sd_b1 == 0.25) |> pull(prop_gt30)
p90_normal_25    <- pp_effect_stats |>
  filter(dist_b1 == "normal",   sd_b1 == 0.25) |> pull(p90)
prop_normal_25   <- pp_effect_stats |>
  filter(dist_b1 == "normal",   sd_b1 == 0.25) |> pull(prop_gt30)
# Cauchy at sd = 0.75 is the closest available grid value to sqrt(2)/2 ~ 0.707
prop_cauchy_jzs  <- pp_effect_stats |>
  filter(dist_b1 == "cauchy",   sd_b1 == 0.75) |> pull(prop_gt30)
fold_excess      <- prop_cauchy_jzs / prop_logistic_25
```

We use the 90th percentile of $|\delta p|$ as the primary plausibility summary: 90% of the prior predictive mass falls below this value, so a lower 90th percentile indicates more conservative prior assumptions about effect sizes. At $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.25$, the scale we recommend as default, the Logistic(0, 0.25) gives a 90th percentile of `r round(p90_logistic_25, 2)` and the Normal(0, 0.25) gives `r round(p90_normal_25, 2)`. Both concentrate mass well within the range of plausible accuracy differences. Concretely, only `r round(prop_logistic_25, 1)`% of the Logistic prior's mass falls above $|\delta p| = 0.30$, an accuracy difference that would already be considered large in most cognitive paradigms. The $\mathrm{Cauchy}(0, \sqrt{2}/2)$, the JZS-motivated default, assigns `r round(prop_cauchy_jzs, 1)`% of its mass to effects this large -- a `r round(fold_excess, 1)`-fold excess relative to the recommended prior that reflects primarily its much larger scale (0.707 vs. 0.25) rather than the Cauchy family per se; as Step 3 shows, family differences are negligible once scales are matched. We return to the BF calibration consequences below. Values up to $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.50$ remain defensible when very large effects are plausible. We recommend $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} \in [0.25, 0.30]$ as the default range when continuous predictors are scaled to $\mathrm{SD} = 1$ and categorical predictors use sum-to-zero $\pm 1$ coding.

### Random Effects Hyperprior {#sec-pp-re}

```{r}
#| label: setup-re-pp
#| include: false

re_pp_summaries <- tryCatch(
  readRDS(here("output", "prior_predictive_re_summaries.rds")),
  error = function(e) NULL
)
re_pp_draws <- tryCatch(
  readRDS(here("output", "prior_predictive_re_draws.rds")),
  error = function(e) NULL
)

re_pp_available <- !is.null(re_pp_summaries) && !is.null(re_pp_draws)
```

For hierarchical models, the hyperprior on the random-effects SD $\sigma$ controls between-subject variability. Candidate hyperpriors should limit $\sigma$ to positive real values. Any principled hyperprior for a SD parameter should have three properties: (a) mode at zero, expressing a preference for parsimony; (b) monotonically decreasing density, so that larger variability receives progressively less prior support; and (c) a scale calibrated to domain knowledge about plausible between-subject variability [@gelman2006; @simpson2017]. Some distributions, like the Exponential, fulfill these properties naturally; others, such as the Student-*t* or Normal distribution, can be truncated to fulfill them (resulting in a half-Student-*t* or half-Normal distribution).

To assess the effects of distribution family and scale, we compared four hyperprior families -- Exponential, Gamma(shape = 2), Half-Normal, and Half-Student-*t*(df = 3) -- at five values of the hyperprior mean (0.15, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00, and 2.50; see Supplementary Materials for details). The Half-Student-*t* is included primarily as a reference point for comparison with the `brms` default `student_t(3, 0, 2.5)` (mean $\approx$ 2.76), which corresponds to the largest scale level. We included the Gamma because it is a widely used hyperprior family in Bayesian modeling practice, and because its positive mode (when shape > 1) provides a deliberate contrast to the three other families.

```{r}
#| label: fig-re-1-p-intercept
#| fig-cap: "Subject-level success probabilities by hyperprior family and hyperprior
#|   mean (rows: mean = 0.15, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00, 2.50; columns: random-effects
#|   structure). At each mean value, all four families produce nearly identical
#|   distributions; the primary driver is the scale parameter, not the family.
#|   Dashed vertical lines mark p = 0.05 and p = 0.95."
#| fig-height: 10
#| fig-width: 12
#| eval: !expr re_pp_available

re_pp_draws |>
  group_by(hp_family, hp_mean, re_structure) |>
  slice_sample(n = 5000) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(hp_mean_label = paste0("mu == ", hp_mean)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = p_intercept, y = hp_family,
             fill = hp_family, colour = hp_family)) +
  geom_density_ridges(stat = "binline", bins = 80,
                      alpha = 0.5, scale = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(0.05, 0.95), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.5) +
  facet_grid(hp_mean_label ~ re_structure,
             labeller = labeller(hp_mean_label = label_parsed)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "D", end = 0.85, guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "D", end = 0.85, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expression("Success probability" ~ italic(p)),
    limits = c(0, 1), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.02))
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete("Family") +
  prior_theme() +
  theme(strip.text.y = element_text(size = 10))
```

When matched by their mean value, all four families produce nearly identical prior predictive distributions for subject-level success probabilities (@fig-re-1-p-intercept). The primary driver is the scale, not the family. At a hyperprior mean of 0.25, subject-level probabilities are well concentrated away from floor and ceiling. In sum, we recommend $\mathrm{Exponential}(4)$ (mean = 0.25) as the default because it requires only one parameter. In `brms` [@burkner2017; @burkner2018] -- an R package for Bayesian multilevel modeling -- the default hyperprior `student_t(3, 0, 2.5)` (mean $\approx$ 2.76) spreads subject-level probabilities too heavily toward 0 and 1 for typical experiments (see the Supplementary Figures in the Supplementary Materials for the implied heterogeneity of condition effects).

## Step 3: Bayes Factor Calibration {#sec-bf-calibration}

The prior predictive analysis established which prior families and scales produce plausible distributions on the probability scale. We now verify that these priors also yield well-calibrated Bayes factors -- that is, Bayes factors that correctly favor $H_1$ when an effect exists and $H_0$ when it does not.[^calibsense] BF estimates can diverge substantially across computational implementations [@pfister2024; @oberauer2026bf]; formal consistency properties provide a check that is implementation-independent. Because the Bayes factor is a ratio of marginal likelihoods, each averaging the likelihood over the prior, a prior that spreads mass across implausibly large effects dilutes this average with regions of low likelihood. The model under $H_1$ is then penalized for this wasted prior mass even when a true effect exists, producing Bayes factors that systematically favor $H_0$.

[^calibsense]: We use *calibration* in the sense of operating characteristics: the rates at which the BF crosses evidence thresholds under known data-generating values of $b_1$. This differs from simulation-based calibration of Bayes factors [@schad2023], which evaluates BF behavior averaged over parameter values drawn from the prior rather than at fixed true values; the two perspectives are complementary.

### Simulation Design

We simulated data under the logit link with true effects ranging from $b_1 = 0$ (null) to $b_1 = 0.50$, crossing three sample sizes (30, 60, 100) and two trial counts (20, 50) in the simulations with three effect prior families (Normal, Logistic, Cauchy) at two scales ($\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.25$ and $0.50$), and two intercept prior families in the GLMs fitted to the data. The two intercept prior families were a matched Logistic(0, 0.75), whose family mirrors the logit link, and a misfit Normal(0, 0.75), which does not. Results for this misfit prior characterize one specific non-matched family at one scale; they need not generalize to all non-matched intercept priors. A second simulation held fixed-effects priors at the recommended matched configuration and varied only the random-effects hyperprior across three candidates: Student-*t*(3, 0, 2.5), Gamma(2, 4), and Exponential(4). Bayes factors were computed via the Savage-Dickey density ratio [@wagenmakers2018; @ly2016] -- the ratio of the prior density to the posterior density at $b_1 = 0$, which estimates how much the data have shifted belief away from the null.[^sdr]

[^sdr]: The Savage-Dickey ratio is valid when three conditions hold: (a) the prior on $b_1$ under $H_1$ is continuous (no point masses), (b) the null hypothesis specifies $b_1 = 0$ as a nested point constraint within the full model, and (c) the prior on nuisance parameters $\eta$ under $H_1$, evaluated at $b_1 = 0$, equals the prior on $\eta$ under $H_0$ -- i.e., $\pi_{H_1}(\eta \mid b_1 = 0) = \pi_{H_0}(\eta)$ [@ly2016; @rouder2012]. All three conditions are satisfied here: the matched logistic and normal priors are continuous, $H_0 : b_1 = 0$ is a point-mass restriction within the full model, and the random-intercept prior is identical under $H_0$ and $H_1$. When the posterior is far from zero, bridge sampling is preferred to avoid kernel density estimation underflow at the null [@oberauer2026bf], as used in the consistency analysis below. We present results for small to moderate effects ($b_1 \in \{0, 0.10, 0.20\}$) in the most data-sparse scenario ($N = 30$, 20 trials), where prior sensitivity is most visible; results for the full range of effect sizes and sample sizes are available in the Supplementary Materials.

```{r}
#| label: setup-bf-raw
#| include: false

bf_raw <- tryCatch({
  results_dir <- here("output", "Simulation_BFCalibration")
  cond_files <- list.files(results_dir, pattern = "^BFCalib_Cond-",
                           full.names = TRUE)
  raw_rows <- lapply(cond_files, function(f) {
    d <- readRDS(f)
    bf10_vec <- d$results[, "BF10"]
    cbind(
      as.data.frame(as.list(d$condition)),
      data.frame(BF10 = bf10_vec, log10_BF10 = log10(bf10_vec))
    )
  })
  bind_rows(raw_rows)
}, error = function(e) NULL)
bf_raw_available <- !is.null(bf_raw)

bf_raw_re <- tryCatch({
  results_dir_re <- here("output", "Simulation_BFCalibration_RE")
  cond_files_re <- list.files(results_dir_re, pattern = "^BFCalib_RE_Cond-",
                              full.names = TRUE)
  raw_rows_re <- lapply(cond_files_re, function(f) {
    d <- readRDS(f)
    bf10_vec <- d$results[, "BF10"]
    cbind(
      as.data.frame(as.list(d$condition)),
      data.frame(BF10 = bf10_vec, log10_BF10 = log10(bf10_vec))
    )
  })
  bind_rows(raw_rows_re)
}, error = function(e) NULL)
bf_raw_re_available <- !is.null(bf_raw_re)

# JZS and Consistency simulations use qs2 format; load from pre-processed flat files
# (generated by scripts/04c and 04d)
bf_raw_jzs <- tryCatch(
  readRDS(here("output", "bf_calibration_jzs_raw.rds")),
  error = function(e) NULL
)
bf_raw_jzs_available <- !is.null(bf_raw_jzs)

```

### Effect of Prior Family and Width

Because we use sum-to-zero $\pm 1$ contrast coding, the BF does not depend on which group is labeled $+1$ or $-1$ -- the implied prior on $|\delta p|$ is symmetric by construction. Given this measurement invariance, the results below isolate the effect of prior family and scale on BF calibration.

```{r}
#| label: fig-bf-violin-family
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of log10(BF10) by effect prior family and width for
#|   N = 30 subjects and 20 trials per subject. Rows (top to bottom): true effect
#|   b1 = 0 (null), b1 = 0.10, b1 = 0.20. Columns: prior SD (0.25 or 0.50).
#|   The choice of prior family has minimal impact; prior width shifts the
#|   distribution modestly. Black dots indicate medians. Dashed lines mark
#|   BF10 = 3 and BF10 = 1/3."
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 10
#| eval: !expr bf_raw_available

bf_raw |>
  filter(
    dist_b0 == "logistic",
    true_b1 %in% c(0, 0.10, 0.20),
    n_subjects == 30,
    n_trials == 20
  ) |>
  mutate(
    dist_b1 = factor(dist_b1, levels = c("cauchy", "normal", "logistic"),
                     labels = c("Cauchy", "Normal", "Logistic")),
    true_b1_label = factor(paste0("b[1] == ", true_b1)),
    sd_b1_label   = factor(paste0("sd[b[1]] == ", sd_b1))
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = dist_b1, y = log10_BF10, fill = dist_b1)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(log10(3), log10(1/3)), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.6, scale = "width", width = 0.8) +
  stat_summary(fun = median, geom = "point", size = 2, colour = "black") +
  facet_grid(true_b1_label ~ sd_b1_label, labeller = label_parsed) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d("Prior family", option = "D", end = 0.85) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expression(BF[10]),
    breaks = log10(c(1/100, 1/10, 1/3, 1, 3, 10, 100)),
    labels = c("1/100", "1/10", "1/3", "1", "3", "10", "100")
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-2.5, 2.5)) +
  labs(x = "Effect prior family") +
  prior_theme()
```

The choice of effect prior family has little impact on the BF (@fig-bf-violin-family). All three families produce nearly identical BF distributions. The prior width has a modest effect: narrower priors ($\mathrm{sd} = 0.25$) yield slightly stronger evidence for $H_1$ when the effect is present, particularly at $b_1 = 0.2$, though the difference is small relative to variability across replications.

### Comparison to the JZS/Cauchy Default

A key desideratum for principled prior choice in hypothesis testing is that the prior should concentrate its mass on scientifically plausible effect sizes. The prior predictive analysis in Step 2 showed that the JZS/Cauchy default fails this desideratum -- its heavy tails assign substantial probability to $|\delta p| > 0.30$ even at moderate scales. Here we characterize what different prior scales and families imply for BF behavior, separating the contribution of scale from that of family.

BF calibration is, at its core, a signal-detection problem: the Bayes factor must discriminate datasets generated under a true effect from datasets generated under the null, and any fixed evidence threshold (such as $\mathrm{BF} > 3$) is simply a decision criterion imposed on a continuous statistic. This framing separates two questions that a single-threshold analysis conflates: how well the BF *discriminates* effects from null (its sensitivity), and *where* a chosen threshold places the decision boundary (its criterion). This separation is precisely what a calibration analysis of the prior needs. The question that matters for prior choice is not whether one prior detects effects better than another, but whether changing the prior moves the decision boundary, and by how much. A signal-detection lens isolates the part of BF behavior a prior can change (the criterion) from the part it cannot (the discriminability of the data). A receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis answers both at once -- it traces the trade-off between true- and false-positive rates across all thresholds, so the curve characterizes discriminability while any single threshold is one operating point on it. We therefore present the calibration results as an ROC analysis.[^h0detect]

[^h0detect]: We report both detection directions. For *$H_1$ detection*, a true positive is $\mathrm{BF}_{10} > t$ when an effect is present ($b_1 \neq 0$) and a false positive is $\mathrm{BF}_{10} > t$ when $b_1 = 0$. For *$H_0$ detection*, the roles reverse: a true positive is $\mathrm{BF}_{01} > t$ when the null holds ($b_1 = 0$) and a false positive is $\mathrm{BF}_{01} > t$ when an effect is present.

To separate the contribution of prior scale from prior family, we ran a factorial simulation crossing two prior scales ($\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} \in \{0.25,\, \sqrt{2}/2 \approx 0.707\}$) with three true effect sizes ($b_1 \in \{0, 0.10, 0.20\}$), three sample sizes ($N \in \{20, 30, 50\}$), and two trial counts (20 or 50 per subject), yielding 36 design conditions. The scale $\sqrt{2}/2$ is the JZS/Cauchy default [@rouder2009; @rouder2012], also used for logistic regression by @oberauer2019. Within each condition, both prior families (Logistic, Cauchy) were fitted to the same simulated datasets, so family comparisons are within-dataset, yielding four effect-prior conditions in total. All four used the matched Logistic(0, 0.75) intercept prior and Exponential(4) random-effects hyperprior. We ran 300 replications per design condition and estimated Bayes factors via the Savage-Dickey density ratio. The full distributions of $\log_{10}(\mathrm{BF}_{10})$ across the design space are shown in the Supplementary Materials.

There is a principled reason to expect the prior scale to leave discriminability unchanged. For a scalar point-null test, the BF under any fixed, symmetric, continuous prior is -- to a good approximation -- a monotone function of a common evidence statistic: how far the likelihood is displaced from the null relative to its dispersion. Because an ROC curve depends only on how a statistic *orders* the datasets, monotone transformations of the same statistic trace out the same curve. What the choice of prior scale *can* do is move a given BF threshold to a different operating point along that shared curve. The simulation tests this expectation and, beyond it, locates each prior's operating point. @fig-bf-jzs-roc plots the full ROC curve -- the true-positive rate against the false-positive rate across all thresholds -- for both $H_1$ detection ($\mathrm{BF}_{10} > t$) and $H_0$ detection ($\mathrm{BF}_{01} > t$).

```{r}
#| label: fig-bf-jzs-roc
#| fig-cap: "Bootstrapped ROC curves (500 resamples, 95\\% CI ribbons) for
#|   $H_1$ detection ($\\mathrm{BF}_{10} > t$; left panel) and $H_0$ detection
#|   ($\\mathrm{BF}_{01} > t$; right panel) across all design conditions.
#|   Color encodes the effect-prior condition (family $\\times$ scale): dark
#|   colors (purple, blue) indicate scale 0.25; light colors (teal, green)
#|   indicate scale 0.707. Linetype encodes the true effect size (solid:
#|   $b_1 = 0.1$; dashed: $b_1 = 0.2$). At matched scales, Logistic and Cauchy
#|   curves nearly overlap. Open circles mark the $\\mathrm{BF} = 3$ operating
#|   point. Row labels: $N$ subjects; column labels: $T$ trials per condition."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| eval: !expr roc_dual_fig_available
knitr::include_graphics(here("figures", "roc_dual_comparison.png"))
```

@fig-bf-jzs-roc confirms this expectation. Within each scale group, Logistic and Cauchy ROC curves nearly overlap in both detection directions. The $\mathrm{BF} = 3$ operating points (open circles), however, shift systematically with scale: relative to scale 0.25, scale 0.707 sits higher on the $H_0$-detection curve and lower on the $H_1$-detection curve. This is the criterion shift made concrete. To make it tangible at the $\mathrm{BF} = 3$ threshold most researchers actually use, consider $N = 30$ and 50 trials: the wider scale supports the null more readily under a true null ($P(\mathrm{BF}_{01} > 3) \approx 0.97$ versus $\approx 0.88$ at scale 0.25) but detects fewer small effects (at $b_1 = 0.10$, $P(\mathrm{BF}_{10} > 3) \approx 23\%$ versus $\approx 34\%$ at scale 0.25). These numbers are not separate findings about the two priors; they are the *same* curve read off at one conventional operating point, the two ends of a single trade-off rather than two different curves. Critically, the ROC curves themselves -- and therefore the area under the ROC curve (AUC) -- do not differ across priors. @fig-bf-jzs-auc quantifies this directly.

```{r}
#| label: fig-bf-jzs-auc
#| fig-cap: "Area under the ROC curve (AUC; Wilcoxon estimator) with 95\\%
#|   bootstrap CI ($n = 500$) for discriminating true effects ($H_1$) from the
#|   null ($H_0$), as a function of sample size. Color encodes prior scale;
#|   linetype encodes prior family. Rows: true effect size; columns: trials per
#|   condition. The dashed horizontal line marks AUC $= 0.50$ (chance). All four
#|   prior combinations produce indistinguishable AUC trajectories; discriminability
#|   is driven by sample size, trial count, and effect size alone. Because
#|   $\\mathrm{BF}_{01} = 1/\\mathrm{BF}_{10}$, the AUC for $H_0$ detection is
#|   identical, so a single panel characterizes both directions."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| eval: !expr auc_fig_available
knitr::include_graphics(here("figures", "auc_jzs_comparison.png"))
```

Across all conditions, and as the monotonicity argument predicts, all four prior combinations produce indistinguishable AUC trajectories; the scale-to-scale differences are non-systematic and lie within bootstrap confidence intervals. Because $\mathrm{BF}_{01} = 1/\mathrm{BF}_{10}$, ranking datasets by evidence for $H_0$ is the exact reverse of ranking them by evidence for $H_1$, so a single AUC characterizes both detection directions. The factors that increase AUC are sample size, trial count, and true effect size: properties of the data, not of the prior. This decomposition has a direct interpretation in signal detection theory: prior scale is a criterion parameter, not a sensitivity parameter. It controls where the BF mass falls relative to any fixed decision threshold, setting the operating point on the ROC curve, but leaves the underlying discriminability of the test unchanged. Prior family contributes negligibly even to this criterion shift. Researchers who prefer the Cauchy family can adopt Cauchy(0, 0.25) and achieve BF behavior that is indistinguishable from the matched Logistic prior at the same scale. The advantage of matched priors is not superior discriminability over other prior families. It is that the prior predictive workflow provides a principled basis for scale selection, grounded in what the prior implies on the probability scale.

### Effect of Random-Effects Hyperprior

```{r}
#| label: fig-bf-violin-re
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of log10(BF10) by random-effects hyperprior for
#|   N = 30, 20 trials. All three hyperpriors produce nearly identical BF
#|   distributions. Black dots indicate medians. Dashed lines mark BF10 = 3
#|   and BF10 = 1/3."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 8
#| eval: !expr bf_raw_re_available

bf_raw_re |>
  filter(
    true_b1 %in% c(0, 0.10, 0.20),
    n_subjects == 30,
    n_trials == 20
  ) |>
  mutate(
    sd_prior_re = factor(sd_prior_re,
                         levels = c("default", "gamma", "exponential"),
                         labels = c("Student-t(3,0,2.5)", "Gamma(2,4)",
                                    "Exponential(4)")),
    true_b1_label = factor(paste0("b[1] == ", true_b1))
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = sd_prior_re, y = log10_BF10, fill = sd_prior_re)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(log10(3), log10(1/3)), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "grey50", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.6, scale = "width", width = 0.7) +
  stat_summary(fun = median, geom = "point", size = 2, colour = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ true_b1_label, nrow = 1, labeller = label_parsed) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d("RE hyperprior", option = "D", end = 0.85) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expression(BF[10]),
    breaks = log10(c(1/100, 1/10, 1/3, 1, 3, 10, 100)),
    labels = c("1/100", "1/10", "1/3", "1", "3", "10", "100")
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-2.5, 2.5)) +
  labs(x = "Random-effects hyperprior") +
  prior_theme() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

The random-effects hyperprior (i.e., the prior on the standard deviation of individual differences) has no meaningful impact on Bayes factors for the fixed effect (@fig-bf-violin-re), even though the Student-*t*(3, 0, 2.5) is far wider than Exponential(4). This nuisance-parameter robustness arises because the BF is a ratio of marginal likelihoods: the random intercepts $u_j$ are present under both $H_1$ and $H_0$ and are marginalized out in each. The common random-effects structure therefore largely cancels in the ratio, leaving the BF dominated by the fixed-effect signal-to-noise ratio. This confirms that the random-effects hyperprior choice has little influence on the Bayes factor for the fixed effect of interest, though it may matter for other inferential goals (e.g., estimating the random-effects variance itself).

### Summary

The most important prior-choice factor across all conditions is the scale of the effect prior; the choice of prior family, intercept prior, and random-effects hyperprior had negligible effects. The signal-detection analysis locates *why*: prior scale acts as a decision criterion, not as a sensitivity parameter. At matched scales, Logistic and Cauchy priors achieve identical discriminability (equal AUC); changing the scale only slides the operating point along the shared ROC curve.

The criterion interpretation makes the practical trade-off explicit. The wider JZS/Cauchy scale places more mass on large effects, setting a strict implicit criterion for $H_1$: when a small genuine effect is present ($b_1 = 0.10$), it frequently produces misleading evidence for $H_0$ -- the diffuse prior makes even a true small effect look like noise. The narrower matched scale is more sensitive to such effects, but correspondingly yields weaker evidence for a true null, correctly supporting $H_0$ less often when no effect is present (full detection and null-support rates across all conditions are in the Supplementary Materials). Both behaviors are two operating points on the same curve; which to favor is a decision about acceptable error rates, not a property of the prior family. For medium and large effects ($b_1 \geq 0.20$), all priors perform similarly.

The formal properties of matched-prior BFs can be stated precisely. Model selection (Lindley) consistency holds for any proper prior with positive density at the true parameter value: as the sample grows, the BF increasingly favors the correct hypothesis [@bayarri2012]. Monotone growth of evidence with effect size at fixed sample size is demonstrated empirically in the supplementary consistency analysis. What matched priors add, then, is not sharper BF calibration but a substantively interpretable basis for choosing the prior scale. Still, when computing Bayes factors for drawing inferences, we recommend running a prior sensitivity analysis by computing BFs across a small set of plausible effect-prior widths (e.g., $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} \in \{0.15, 0.25, 0.35\}$) to test the robustness of the results over varying prior scales.

### Transparency and Openness

[All simulation scripts, model-fitting code, and analysis code reported in this paper are publicly available at <https://github.com/GidonFrischkorn/PriorsBinomial>, and the pre-computed simulation outputs needed to reproduce the figures and tables are archived on the Open Science Framework (<https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/YJGKT>).]{.content-visible unless-meta="mask"}[All simulation scripts, model-fitting code, and analysis code reported in this paper are publicly available in a public repository, with pre-computed simulation outputs archived on the Open Science Framework (links withheld for masked review).]{.content-visible when-meta="mask"} No empirical data were collected; all results are based on computer simulations. This study was not preregistered. [An earlier, more tutorial-oriented version of this manuscript is publicly available as a preprint [@frischkorn2026preprint]; that version was submitted to *Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science* and not accepted. The present manuscript is a substantially revised and extended version produced in response to the recommendations from that review.]{.content-visible unless-meta="mask"}[An earlier, more tutorial-oriented version of this manuscript is available as a preprint; bibliographic details are withheld for masked review.]{.content-visible when-meta="mask"} Software versions used: R 4.5.2 [@r2023]; SimDesign 2.25 [@chalmers2020]; brms 2.23.0 [@burkner2017; @burkner2018]; CmdStan 2.38.0 [@stan2023] via cmdstanr 0.9.0 [@gabry2024]; bridgesampling 1.2.1 [@gronau2017].

## Recommendations {#sec-recommendations}

The defaults below are specific to binomial GLMs. They rest on the simulations in Steps 2 and 3; scale recommendations for other model families -- ordinal, count, lognormal -- would require dedicated simulation work and are beyond the scope of this paper.

Researchers who want to apply the recommendations directly without reading the derivations can use the defaults in @tbl-prior-summary and the ready-to-use code below.

| Parameter | Link | Recommended prior | Key property |
|:----------|:-----|:------------------|:-------------|
| Intercept ($b_0$) | logit | $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 0.75)$ | $< 10\%$ prior mass in floor/ceiling region |
| Intercept ($b_0$) | probit | $\mathrm{Normal}(0, 0.75)$ | same |
| Effect ($b_1$) | logit | $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, s)$, $s$ = 0.25–0.30 | 90th percentile of $|\delta p| \approx$ 0.29–0.34 |
| Effect ($b_1$) | probit | $\mathrm{Normal}(0, s)$, $s$ = 0.25–0.30 | 90th percentile of $|\delta p| \approx$ 0.27–0.32 |
| RE hyperprior ($\sigma_u$) | both | $\mathrm{Exponential}(4)$ | mean $= 0.25$ link-scale units |

: Summary of default prior recommendations for binomial GLMs. All values assume sum-to-zero $\pm 1$ coding and predictors scaled to $\mathrm{SD} = 1$; see *From Defaults to Informed Priors* below for other coding schemes. {#tbl-prior-summary}

The three workflow steps converge on a coherent set of recommendations. Priors shape Bayes factors, but priors also encode prior beliefs about plausible effects, so their implications on the probability scale must be examined rather than assumed. Choosing overly wide priors (as commonly done with "weakly informative" defaults) biases BFs toward the null and implies implausible prior beliefs about effect sizes on the probability scale. Our recommended priors satisfy both criteria simultaneously: They produce plausible prior predictive distributions on the probability scale (Step 2) and well-calibrated Bayes factors across a realistic range of effects (Step 3). Yet the converse does not hold: Some priors that happen to yield adequate BF calibration may still imply implausible effect sizes (e.g., the Cauchy).

Researchers with no domain-specific prior knowledge can use the default priors directly. If prior information about expected effect sizes is available, the prior scale can be adjusted accordingly. When the predictor's SD differs from 1, the default prior scale should be adapted using the scaling formulas below.

### Default Prior Specifications

@tbl-intercept-prior and @tbl-effect-prior show our recommendation for default priors for intercept and effect parameters in binomial GLMs. The hyperprior on random-effect SDs should concentrate mass below approximately 0.5 on the logit scale (corresponding to between-subject differences in success probability of roughly 10--15 percentage points around typical baselines). Our recommended default is `exponential(4)` (mean = 0.25). For larger expected between-subject variability, `exponential(2)` (mean = 0.50) provides a wider alternative while remaining well calibrated.

| Link | Recommended prior | Rationale |
|------|------------------|-----------|
| logit | $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 0.75)$ | Matched family; sd = 0.75 gives < 10% floor/ceiling mass |
| probit | $\mathrm{Normal}(0, 0.75)$ | Matched family; same criterion |

: Recommended intercept priors for binomial GLMs. {#tbl-intercept-prior}

| Link | Recommended prior | Rationale |
|------|------------------|-----------|
| logit | $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, s)$, $s$ = 0.25–0.30 | Matched family; 90th percentile of $|\delta p| \approx$ 0.29–0.34 |
| probit | $\mathrm{Normal}(0, s)$, $s$ = 0.25–0.30 | Matched family; 90th percentile of $|\delta p| \approx$ 0.27–0.32 |

: Recommended effect priors for binomial GLMs. {#tbl-effect-prior}

#### Ready-to-use brms prior specifications

```{r}
#| label: recommended-priors
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

# Logit link (logistic regression)
logit_priors <- c(
  brms::prior(logistic(0, 0.75), class = Intercept),
  brms::prior(logistic(0, 0.25), class = b)
)

# Probit link (probit regression)
probit_priors <- c(
  brms::prior(normal(0, 0.75), class = Intercept),
  brms::prior(normal(0, 0.25), class = b)
)
```

The code uses the effect-prior scale 0.25, the lower end of the recommended range of 0.25 to 0.30.[^centered]

[^centered]: In `brms`, a prior with `class = Intercept` applies to the intercept of the internally *centered* design matrix. Under balanced sum-to-zero $\pm 1$ coding, the centered intercept coincides with the design intercept, so the matched intercept prior applies unchanged; for unbalanced designs or other codings, the `0 + Intercept` formula syntax places the prior on the design intercept directly.

For models with subject-level random effects:

```{r}
#| label: recommended-priors-re
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

# Logit link with random intercept
logit_priors_re <- c(
  brms::prior(logistic(0, 0.75), class = Intercept),
  brms::prior(logistic(0, 0.25), class = b),
  brms::prior(exponential(4),    class = sd)
)
```

### From Defaults to Informed Priors {#sec-scaling}

Informed priors can be generated by adjusting the defaults in two ways. First, the prior can be made wider or narrower (i.e., increasing or decreasing $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1}$) depending on whether one expects larger or smaller effects. Second, the prior width must be rescaled if the predictor's SD differs from 1. The recommended $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} \in [0.25, 0.30]$ assumes $\mathrm{SD}(x) = 1$ (as obtained by sum-to-zero $\pm 1$ coding of a binary predictor). When the predictor's SD differs from 1, the scale of the prior should be rescaled accordingly:

$$\mathrm{sd}_{b_1}(\mathrm{predictor SD} = s) = \frac{\mathrm{sd}_{b_1}(\mathrm{SD} = 1)}{s}$$

Smaller predictor SDs require a *wider* prior on $b_1$; larger predictor SDs require a *narrower* prior. Here are some examples:

- $\pm 0.5$ coding ($s = 0.5$): use $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.25 / 0.5 = 0.50$
- $\pm 1/\sqrt{2}$ orthonormal ($s \approx 0.707$): use $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.25 / 0.707 \approx 0.354$
- $0/1$ treatment coding ($s = 0.5$ in a balanced design): use $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.25 / 0.5 = 0.50$
- Standardized continuous predictor ($s = 1$): use $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.25$ directly

For the no-intercept parameterization (`0 + condition` in `brms`), see [Appendix B](#sec-appendix-no-intercept).

#### Dealing with multi-level factors

When a factor has more than two levels, we recommend using theoretically meaningful orthogonal contrasts that reflect specific hypotheses. These could either be a set of orthogonal contrasts that breaks down factors with more levels into a sub-set of contrasts with only two levels, or transforming factors into continuous predictors when this is reasonable.

For researchers who do not have specific directional hypotheses but want to test the omnibus question "does the factor have any effect?", orthonormal contrasts provide a principled alternative. Orthonormal contrasts [e.g., `contr.equalprior()` from the `bayestestR` package; @makowski2019; @rouder2012] equalize contrast column variances, ensuring that the prior width is the same for every pairwise comparison. In a balanced design with $J$ levels, each column has predictor $\mathrm{SD} = 1 / \sqrt{J}$, giving the rescaling formula:

$$\mathrm{sd}_{b_1}(\mathrm{orthonormal, } J \mathrm{ levels}) = 0.25 \times \sqrt{J}$$

See @tbl-orthonormal-scaling in [Appendix A](#sec-appendix-contrasts) for a look-up table of rescaled prior widths for common multi-level and factorial designs.

**In short:** use $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 0.75)$ for the intercept, $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 0.25)$ for the effect, and $\mathrm{Exponential}(4)$ for the random-effects SD when applying logistic regression; substitute $\mathrm{Normal}$ priors with the same scales for probit regression. Rescale the effect prior width when the predictor SD differs from 1, and report BFs across $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} \in \{0.15, 0.25, 0.35\}$ as a sensitivity check.

```{r}
#| label: fig-orthonormal-demo
#| fig-cap: "Prior predictive distribution of a single representative
#|   pairwise $\\delta p$ under orthonormal contrasts with the rescaled prior
#|   ($\\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.25 \\times \\sqrt{J}$). The distribution is
#|   nearly identical across $J$ -- confirming that the scaling rule
#|   preserves per-comparison calibration."
#| fig-height: 4.5
#| fig-width: 6
#| message: false

set.seed(2024)
n_draws <- 50000

demo_list <- lapply(c(2, 3, 4), function(J) {
  oc <- orthonormal_contrasts(J)
  sd_b1_scaled <- 0.25 * oc$scaling_factor

  b0 <- rlogis(n_draws, location = 0, scale = 0.75)
  B <- matrix(
    rnorm(n_draws * (J - 1), mean = 0, sd = sd_b1_scaled),
    nrow = n_draws, ncol = J - 1
  )

  eta_mat <- outer(b0, rep(0, J)) + B %*% t(oc$Q)
  p_mat   <- ilogit(eta_mat)

  delta_p_pair <- p_mat[, 1] - p_mat[, 2]

  data.frame(
    J            = paste0("J = ", J),
    delta_p_pair = delta_p_pair
  )
})

demo_draws <- do.call(rbind, demo_list)

p_single <- ggplot(demo_draws, aes(x = delta_p_pair,
                                    y = J, fill = J)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.5, scale = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-0.20, -0.10, 0.10, 0.20),
             linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.4,
             colour = "grey40") +
  scale_x_continuous(expression(delta * p ~ "(single pair)"),
                     limits = c(-0.8, 0.8)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d("Factor levels", option = "D", end = 0.85) +
  ggtitle("Single pairwise comparison") +
  prior_theme()

p_single
```


## Applying the Workflow Beyond Binomial Models {#sec-extensions}

The three-step workflow is not limited to binomial models. We illustrate here how it transfers to other common model families to demonstrate generality -- not to provide empirically validated scale recommendations, which would require dedicated simulation work for each family. We provide a walkthrough for ordinal regression and lognormal regression and briefly sketch the application to three other common model families.

### Ordinal regression

Ordinal regression is the method of choice when the data consist of frequencies over more than two ordered response categories, for instance, response frequencies from Likert scales. Cumulative logit and probit models use the same link functions as binomial GLMs, applied to cumulative probabilities across ordered response categories. Each threshold parameter $\tau_k$ determines the boundary between adjacent categories on the latent scale, and the inverse link converts these boundaries to cumulative probabilities.

**Step 1 (Matched priors).** The matched prior principle applies directly: a $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, 1)$ prior on each threshold under the cumulative logit link produces a uniform distribution of cumulative probabilities at that threshold. The matched prior ($\mathrm{sd} = 1$) yields approximately uniform category probabilities on average. Note that the effect of scaling differs from the binary case: scaling to $\mathrm{sd} < 1$ clusters all thresholds near the center of the latent scale, which concentrates prior mass *toward* the extreme categories (a U-shaped distribution of category probabilities); scaling to $\mathrm{sd} > 1$ spreads thresholds outward, concentrating mass in the middle categories (a bell-shaped distribution). For the effect prior on predictors, the same matched families apply as in the binomial case.

**Step 2 (Prior predictive simulation).** The key difference from the binomial case is that the probability scale is now a vector of category probabilities rather than a single success probability. Prior predictive simulation should examine the implied distribution of category probabilities and, critically, of contrasts between conditions (e.g., shifts in the most likely category, or changes in cumulative probabilities). One can draw $b_0$, threshold spacings, and effect coefficients from the joint prior, compute category probabilities via the inverse link, and inspect whether the implied distributions match substantive expectations about response patterns.

**Step 3 (BF calibration).** Bayes factor calibration follows the same template: simulate data with known effects, fit cumulative models with the candidate priors, and evaluate BF sensitivity. The Savage-Dickey density ratio applies to the effect coefficient just as in the binomial case, because $b_1 = 0$ is a point null on the same parameter regardless of the outcome distribution, and the density ratio at zero is well-defined whenever a continuous prior is placed on $b_1$.

### Lognormal regression for response times

Response times are among the most common continuous outcomes in experimental psychology. Because they are right-skewed and strictly positive, lognormal regression (`family = lognormal()` in `brms`, with an identity link on the log scale) is a natural modeling choice.
